# MAIN ARTICLE # Protocols # ATP-mediated intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4-based nanozyme: One step detection of blood glucose at physiological pH N.V. Srikanth Vallabani a , Ajay S. Karakoti a , b , ∗ , Sanjay Singh a , ∗ - a Division of Biological and Life Sciences, School of Arts and Sciences, Ahmedabad University, Central Campus, Navrangpura, Ahmedabad380009, Gujarat, India - b School of Engineering and Applied Science, Ahmedabad University, GICT Building, Central Campus, Navrangpura, Ahmedabad380009, Gujarat, India # a r t i c l e i n f o Article history: Received21December2016Received in revised form3February2017Accepted4February2017Available online9February2017 Keywords: Iron-oxide nanoparticles Nanozymes Point-of-care device Horseradish peroxidase Ascorbic acid # 1. Introduction Nanotechnology has given a huge impetus to the quest for mimicking nature. The discovery of nanozymes, nanoparticles possessing enzyme like properties, was one such step in the creation of synthetic analogues of naturally existing enzymes. Unlike natural enzymes, nanomaterials exhibiting enzyme-like activity display higher stability in harsh conditions, lower cost of production, flexible storage conditions and easy manipulation of catalytic activity, which is controlled by shape, size, and composition of nanomaterials [1]. Recently, the intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of Iron Oxide NPs (IONPs) has gained phenomenal interest in catalysis, bio-sensing, immunoassays and degradation of organic pollutants in water [2-5]. It was soon realized that several other nanoparticles, such as Ag, GO, CuO and Au can also exhibit synthetic ∗ Corresponding authors at: Division of Biological and Life Sciences, School of Arts and Sciences, Ahmedabad University, Central Campus, Navrangpura, Ahmedabad380009, Gujarat, India. E-mail addresses: ajay.karakoti@ahduni.edu.in (A.S. Karakoti), sanjay.singh@ahduni.edu.in, sanjaysingh26@gmail.com (S. Singh). # a b s t r a c t Fe3O4nanoparticles (Fe3O4NPs), demonstrating peroxidase-like activity has garnered attention in the detection of several biomolecules, therefore, emerged as an excellent nano-biosensing agent. The intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4NPs at acidic pH is the fundamental action driving the oxidation of substrates like TMB, resulting in a colorimetric product formation used in the detection of biomolecules. Hence, the detection sensitivity essentially depends on the ability of oxidation by Fe3O4NPs in presence of H2O2. However, the limited sensitivity and pH condition constraint have been identified as the major drawbacks in the detection of biomolecules at physiological pH. Herein, we report overwhelming of the fundamental limitation of acidic pH and tuning the peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4NPs at physiological pH by using ATP. In presence of ATP, Fe3O4NPs exhibited enhanced peroxidase-like activity over a wide range of pH and temperatures. Mechanistically, it was found that the ability of ATP to participate in single electron transfer reaction, through complexation with Fe3O4NPs, results in the generation of hydroxyl radicals which are responsible for enhanced peroxidase activity at physiological pH. We utilized this ATP-mediated enhanced peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4NPs for single step detection of glucose with a colorimetric detection limit of50 𝛍 M. Further, we extended this single step detection method to monitor glucose level in human blood serum and detected in a time span of <5min at pH7.4. © 2017Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved. peroxidase-like activity [6-9]. However, the Fenton chemistry driving oxidation of substrates by Fe ions (Fe II/ Fe III ) in presence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) makes IONPs one of the best peroxidase mimics among various nanoparticle types [10]. Despite the investigation on several types of nanomaterials exhibiting peroxidase-like activity, limited success has been obtained in the development of enzyme-mimicking nanoparticle-based detection of analytes in a single step and at physiological pH. This limitation could be due to the lower substrate affinity and specificity and importantly lower catalytic activity and stability at physiological conditions. It is well known that if a detection, imaging or therapeutic system is to be used in biologically relevant conditions, it must be biocompatible and functioning at physiological conditions. Additionally, above issues can also affect the catalytic enactment of nanomaterials and thus hinder the usefulness in the development of a realtime and point-of-care device construction. So far improvement in peroxidase-like activity of nanomaterials at physiological pH is a concern and limited efforts have been explored. Recently, Shah et al. [11] have shown that the peroxidase-like activity of30nm AuNPs can be improved three-folds using a natural biomolecule, ATP. They argued that negatively charged ATP improves the stability Contents lists available at ScienceDirect # Colloids and Surfaces B: Biointerfaces j o ur nal ho me p ag e: www.elsevier.com/locate/colsurfb of oxidized TMB (positively charged), through simple electrostatic attraction, however, did not explore the utility of this effect in the detection of analytes. Moreover, the ionic liquid is also reported to improve the thermal stability of the product but completely inhibits the enzyme-like activity of nanoparticle due to the high viscosity [12]. Therefore, it is imperative to explore new alternatives or boosting agents that can selectively improve the peroxidase-like activity of nanomaterials, which can be translated into the sensitive detection of analytes. The roles of surface capping molecules on the peroxidaselike activity of IONPs are investigated by several investigators worldwide. However, the study is limited to the few surface capping molecules such as citrate, glycine, polyl -lysine, polyethyleneimine, carboxymethyl dextran, heparin and Prussian blue etc. [13,14]. Further, it is also reported that surface coating of IONPs by PEG leads to the drop in intrinsic peroxidase-like activity. This contradictory effect could be rationalized by considering the high surface to volume ratio of nanomaterials, which often strongly and selectively adsorb reactive molecules on the surface from the immediate suspension medium. Such event could hinder/cover the surface of nanomaterials from being accessible to other molecules or ions, resulting in the alteration of activity. Therefore, this preferential interaction can either enhance or inhibit the catalytic activity of nanomaterials. In this context, few metal ions such as Ag + , Bi3+ , and Hg2+ ions are reported to boost the peroxidase-like activity of AuNPs [15]. These ions are not attached to the surface of nanoparticles rather present in the reaction medium. It was suggested that these ions are deposited on AuNPs surface, which may result in the increase in the overall concentration of surface ions on AuNPs, thus enhancing the peroxidase-like activity. Horseradish Peroxidase (HRP) mimetic activity has been shown by several nanomaterials such as Au, Fe3O4, nanosheets (graphene, MoS2, WS2, and CeO2) etc. Using this property several sensors have been designed for the sensitive detection of glucose, cholesterol, pesticides, cellular reactive oxygen species and glutathione [14,16-23]. However, these and other reported approaches for detection of analytes frequently involve a complex, time-consuming and two step procedures. Additionally, the fundamental requirement for exhibiting peroxidase activity by IONPs is the oxidizing burst of radicals at acidic pH that oxidizes TMB into a colored product. This limits the application of peroxidase based bio-sensing at neutral pH and often requires a two-step reaction for detection of biomolecules that compromises the efficiency [14,24-26]. Therefore, it is vital to establish novel methods that can detect analytes within one pot and wide linear range with low detection limit. In this study, we report a novel strategy to overcome the fundamental necessity of acidic pH and show that with the use of ATP, Fe3O4NPs exhibit excellent peroxidase activity at physiological pH. In presence of ATP, the enhanced catalytic activity of Fe3O4NPs can also be tuned from acidic ( ∼ 90%) to alkaline pH (70-80%) when compared to physiological pH (100%). Further, this enhanced peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4NPs was applied directly for a single step detection of glucose at pH7.4. This method could detect glucose linearly from0.05mM to4mM, a physiologically relevant concentration present in human serum. We also show that this method could be successfully applied for detection of glucose in human blood serum in single step and at physiological pH. # 2. Experimental section # 2.1. Materials FeCl3 · 6H2O, FeSO4 · 7H2O, NH4OH, Fructose, Adenosine triphosphate disodium salt (ATP), citric acid monohydrate, L-Ascorbic Fig. 1. Characterization of Fe3O4NPs: Aqueous dispersed citrate coated Fe3O4NPs were characterized by a) TEM imaging, b) XRD pattern, c) SAED pattern and d) FTIR spectra to determine the size, morphology, crystalline structure and presence of citrate group on Fe3O4NPs surface. acid, Phosphate Buffered Saline (PBS) were purchased from HiMedia (Mumbai, India). Glucose solution was purchased from Gibco (Life Technologies (India) Pvt Ltd.), 3,3 ′ ,5,5 ′ -Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was purchased from Acros Organics (Geel, Belgium), Glucose oxidase (GOx, from Aspergillus niger ) was obtained from SigmaAldrich (St. Louis, USA) and30% H2O2was procured from SD fine chemicals (Mumbai, India). All other chemicals purchased were of analytical reagent grade and used as received. # 2.2. Synthesis of Fe3O4NPs The facile synthesis of citrate-coated Fe3O4NPs was performed by following the procedure reported by Racuciu et al. with slight modifications [27]. Briefly, 3.6gm FeCl3 · 6H2O and1.8gm FeSO4 · 7H2O salts were dissolved in80mL MilliQ water. At80 ◦ C,

Table1Comparison of kinetic parameters between Fe3O4NPs (with ATP), horseradish peroxidase (HRP) and other IONPs, where Km is the Michaelis constant and Vmax is the maximum reaction velocity. nitrogen gas was purged into the mixture and20mL of NH4OH was added slowly (the solution turns black). The temperature of80 ◦ C was maintained for further30min with continuous N2bubbling. Later, using an external magnet, the NPs were pelleted down and the supernatant was discarded. The washing step was carried out for4-5times with boiled MilliQ water to remove excess ammonia. To the pellet solution ( ∼ 30mL), 2.5gm of citric acid was mixed and stirred at90 ◦ C for2h. After cooling down to room temperature, Fe3O4NPs were dialyzed for24h in MilliQ water using12KDa dialysis membrane (water was changed every8h). After dialysis NPs were washed by centrifugation at10,000rpm for10min to remove the remnants. In the final step, the Fe3O4NPs solution was centrifuged at4000rpm for6min and the supernatant was collected. # 2.3. Characterization of Fe3O4NPs The particle size was measured using Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) equipped with20-120kV (JEM1400plus, JEOL). Phase identification of Fe3O4NPs was analysed over the2 𝛉 range from20to90degrees using X-ray diffraction (XRD) system (Bruker AXS, D8Discover).The concentration of Fe3O4NPs for zeta potential measurement was1mg/mL in PBS. Further the zeta potential studies of NPs were followed in presence of ATP and ascorbic acid (AA) at pH7.4. For dispersion, this suspension was gently vortexed for20s and zeta potential was determined using Dynamic Light Scattering (Zetasizer Nano-ZS, ZEN3600) Malvern instruments Ltd. Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FTIR) spectra was measured in the range of500-4000cm -1using Spectrum Two FT-IR Spectrometer, L160000A, PerkinElmer. # 2.4. Peroxidase-like activity at pH7.4 Kinetic study was performed in a total reaction volume of200 𝛍 L with different concentrations of Fe3O4NPs (5, 15, 30 𝛍 g mL -1 ) and fixed concentrations of H2O2 (250mM), TMB (1mM) and2.5mM ATP in phosphate buffered saline (Na2HPO4 0.81mM, KCl0.26mM, KH2PO4 0.14mM and NaCl13.6mM, pH7.4) at30 ◦ C. To find out the effect of ATP on the peroxidase-like activity of NPs, the study was performed in presence and absence of ATP. Absorbance was measured at652nm till45min using synergy HT multi-detection microplate reader, BioTek. # 2.5. Kinetic analysis Kinetics of the experiments were performed at30 ◦ C using30 𝛍 g mL -1Fe3O4NPs, 2.5mM ATP and1mM TMB with varying concentrations of H2O2 (5-600mM) and a fixed concentration of H2O2 (250mM) with varying concentrations of TMB (0.1-2mM) in200 𝛍 L of PBS at pH7.4. The absorption was recorded at652nm with30s interval at kinetics mode. The kinetic parameters Km and Vmax were calculated by the Lineweaver-Burk plots, as Where /ETB is the initial velocity, Km is Michaelis constant, Vmax represents the maximal reaction velocity and [s] is the substrate concentration. # 2.6. Effect of temperature and pH on peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4NPs Experiments were carried using30 𝛍 g mL -1Fe3O4NPs, 2.5mM ATP, 1mM TMB and250mM H2O2in200 𝛍 L of PBS. The reactions were carried at different pH (1-12) and a wide range of temperature (20-80 ◦ C) in presence and absence of ATP. For pH reactions, the temperature was maintained at30 ◦ C and temperature studies were carried at pH7.4. The absorbance at652nm was measured after10min of intervals. Thermal stability assay to compare the peroxidase activity of Fe3O4NPs and HRP was performed. For Fe3O4NPs study, the concentrations used were30 𝛍 g mL -1Fe3O4NPs, 1mM TMB, 250mM H2O2and2.5mM ATP. For HRP study, the concentrations were10ng HRP, 0.8mM TMB, and10mM H2O2. The total reaction volume was200 𝛍 L and maintained with PBS at pH7.4. Absorbance was recorded at652nm after5min intervals at37 ◦ C and60 ◦ C, respectively. # 2.7. Antioxidant assay To reveal the role of radicals ( · OH) participation in oxidation of TMB, experiments were carried in presence of30 𝛍 g mL -1Fe3O4NPs, 2.5mM ATP, 1mM TMB, 250mM H2O2and an antioxidant, ascorbic acid (3mM), in PBS at pH7.4at30 ◦ C. The absorbance was read at652nm after45min of incubation. Corresponding control experiments were run either in absence of AA or ATP under identical experimental conditions. # 2.8. Glucose detection at pH7.4 For glucose detection, the reaction contained different concentrations of glucose (0.05-4mM), 30 𝛍 g mL -1Fe3O4NPs, 1mM TMB, 2.5mM ATP and GOx (10Units) in200 𝛍 L of PBS at pH7.4. Absorbance was measured after30min of incubation with continuous shaking at40 ◦ C and60 ◦ C, respectively. For selectivity experiment, fructose (4and6mM) was used instead of glucose. Moreover to check the interaction of glucose in the reaction mixture or to find out the synergistic activity of ATP, controls were run without GOx and ATP under identical experimental conditions as above. # 2.9. Glucose detection in human blood samples Blood ( ∼ 5mL) was collected aseptically from healthy volunteer, serum was collected and centrifuged at12000rpm for10min, and then the supernatant was spiked with glucose to make1.25, 5and10mM respectively. Pure serum supernatant was considered as control. The samples were diluted10times using PBS (pH7.4) and glucose detection in serum was detected using100 𝛍 L serum sample, GOx (10Units), 30 𝛍 g mL -1Fe3O4NPs, 1.5mM ATP and 1mM TMB. The total reaction volume was200 𝛍 L, make up with PBS pH7.4. Finally the solution was diluted20times, thus the tested concentrations of glucose in blood serum were0.0625, 0.25and0.5mM. The absorbance at652nm was measured using UV-vis spectrophotometer. # 3. Results and discussion # 3.1. Characterization of citrate-coated Fe3O4NPs TEM micrographs revealed that the synthesized citrated coated particles are quasi-spherical in shape (Fig. 1a) with an average particle size of13 ± 3.5nm (Fig. 1a inset). The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern showed the characteristic diffraction ring pattern of polycrystalline face-centered cubic (FCC) crystal structure of Fe3O4. Diffraction pattern was indexed [(220), (311), (400), (422), (511) and (440)] which match well with the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the corresponding crystallographic planes (Fig. 1b and c) [28-30]. The average zeta potential of particles dispersed in PBS at pH7.4was found to be -39.8 ± 0.6mV, suggesting the stable colloidal suspension of nanoparticles (ESI Fig. 1). Moreover the presence of ATP and AA showed no significant effect on the stability of NPs (ESI Table1). Although the zeta potential value of NPs decreased from -27.6 ± 2.5mV to -19.7 ± 1.05mV (in presence of ATP) and -18.8 ± 1.2mV (in presence of AA), however we did not observe any agglomeration in particles. FTIR analysis displayed a sharp vibration peak at544cm -1 , which could be ascribed to Fe-O stretching vibrations from Fe3O4. In addition, two more transmission peaks arising at1415and1623cm -1could be respectively, due to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching modes of vibration of COO -from citric acid. The presence of these transmission peaks suggests the presence of citric acid on the surface of Fe3O4NPs. Further, a broad transmission peak centered at3217cm -1 , could be ascribed to the stretching vibrations of O H from citric acid and water molecules (Fig. 1d) [31,32]. # 3.2. Fe3O4NPs exhibit peroxidase-like activity at physiological pH The peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4NPs was evaluated in presence and absence of ATP at physiological pH. As reported earlier by others, similarly Fe3O4NPs did not oxidize the TMB, an indicator of peroxidase activity, at pH7.4 (Fig. ESI2). We followed the reaction kinetics of peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4NPs up to the concentration of30 𝛍 g mL -1 . In general peroxidase-like activity is dictated by · OH radicals, which are generated at acidic pH (Eq. (1)) but not at physiological pH (Eq. (2)). However, in presence of ATP, Fe3O4NPs showed linear oxidation kinetics of TMB at all the three tested concentration (5, 15, 30 𝛍 g mL -1 ) at pH7.4 (Fig. 2a). This established the role of ATP, in mediating the peroxidase mimetic activity of Fe3O4NPs at physiological pH. Mechanistically, it has been well established that Fe II can break H2O2into · OH radicals in presence of ATP at physiological pH (Eq. (3)). Therefore, it is expected that due to the feasibility of above reaction at physiological pH, Fe3O4NPs can catalyse the reduction of H2O2 (in presence of ATP) that in turn oxidizes TMB even at pH7.4. Additionally, reports describe that ATP stabilizes the colored end products of ABTS and TMB, through single electron transfer reactions [1,11,33,34]. It has also been shown, in the case of AuNPs, that ATP can drive the peroxidase activity by stabilizing the colored TMB cation radical upon reaction with hydroxyl radicals ( · OH) [1]. Similarly, the ability of ATP in stabilizing the ABTS and TMB radical cations has been reported in catalyzing the peroxidase activity of DNAzymes [34]. However, most of these experiments were carried out at acidic pH. Fig. 2. Effect of ATP on the peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4NPs at pH7.4: a) Concentration-dependent kinetic study of the peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4NPs (5, 15and30 𝛍 g mL -1 ) in presence of ATP followed for45min b) Schematic diagram showing the comparative peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4NPs at physiological pH in the absence (upper panel) and presence (lower panel) of ATP. Colour change in tubes indicates the oxidation of TMB (blue) by Fe3O4NPs. Data expressed as standard deviation (SD) calculated from three (n = 3) independent experiments. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.) Nonetheless, it has also been reported that ATP has a strong affinity for complexing with Fe II that can generate · OH radicals in presence of H2O2at physiological pH (Eq. (3)) [35]. Therefore, it is expected that the ability of Fe3O4NPs to complex with ATP in presence of H2O2results in a burst release of hydroxyl radicals. It must also be mentioned here that ATP and Fe3O4NPs both must be present for observing the peroxidase activity of Fe3O4NPs. In order to understand whether ATP makes a surface coating on particles surface or it must just be present in the reaction mixture, we pre-exposed Fe3O4NPs with ATP for two hours and then washed thoroughly to remove excess ATP from its surface. We observed that ATP pre-exposed Fe3O4NPs did not show any increase in per- Fig. 3. Study of kinetic parameters (Km and Vmax): (a) The Michaelis-Menten curves for the peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4NPs at fixed concentration of TMB (a) and H2O2 (b). For TMB, the concentration of TMB was1mM and a concentration of H2O2was varied from5to600mM. For H2O2the concentration of H2O2was fixed to250mM while the concentration of TMB was varied from0.1-2mM. Double reciprocal plots of TMB (c) and H2O2 (d) was made from the respective Michaelis-Menten curves. oxidase activity at pH7.4 (data not shown), suggests that ATPFe3O4NPs complex formation, as suggested in Eq. (3), may also occur in presence of H2O2and a minimum threshold concentration of all three reaction intermediates may be necessary to exhibit peroxidase-like activity. A schematic representation displaying no peroxidase-like activity (in absence of ATP), whereas, excellent activity (in presence of ATP) of Fe3O4NPs (5, 15, 30 𝛍 g mL -1 ) at physiological pH was shown in Fig. 2b. The colorless solution (Fig. 2b, upper panel) showing that the particles could not oxidize the TMB solution, whereas intense blue color development was shown in the case of particles in presence of ATP (Fig. 2b, lower panel). # 3.3. Study of kinetic parameters (Km and Vmax) The steady state kinetic studies of TMB oxidation by Fe3O4NPs showed a hyperbolic relationship between the substrate concentration and rate of reaction, like a typical Michaelis-Menten reaction (Fig. 3a). The kinetic parameters Km and Vmax were determined from Lineweaver-Burk plots and compared with literature reported values for HRP. From the kinetic analysis it was found that at pH7.4, Fe3O4NPs (Km = 0.37) showed a higher affinity towards TMB than HRP (Km = 0.43) at acidic pH. It was also found that Km value of Fe3O4NPs for H2O2 (Km = 54.6) was higher than HRP (Km = 3.7), suggesting that Fe3O4NPs require more concentration of H2O2for depicting same peroxidase activity as HRP [2]. The maximum reaction velocity (Vmax) of Fe3O4NPs for TMB and H2O2performed at pH7.4was3.85and4.83times lower than HRP studies reported at pH4, (Fig. 3a-d and Table1). It should be noted here that the maximum studies of HRP are conducted at acidic pH due to limited peroxidase activity at physiological or basic pH. Therefore, the kinetic parameter values for Fe3O4NPs (at physiological pH) were compared to HRP and other IONPs values determined at acidic pH (Table1) [36,37]. It was found that the kinetic parameters in the study were comparable to the HRP and other nanoparticles values at acidic pH. # 3.4. ATP preserves the peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4NPs at a broad range of pH and temperature The effect of temperature on the peroxidase activity of Fe3O4NPs in presence of ATP showed that the catalytic activity of NPs increase with the rise in reaction temperature and maximum activity was observed at60 ◦ C (Fig. 4a and Fig. ESI3a). Further, it was found that ∼ 60% of activity was retained even at80 ◦ C of temperature. However, in absence of ATP, no peroxidase activity of Fe3O4NPs was observed even at higher temperatures. In addition, in presence of ATP, Fe3O4NPs displayed peroxidase-like activity in a broad range of pH (2-11) with maximum activity at pH7.4 (Fig. 4b and Fig. ESI3b). This observation further suggests that in presence of ATP, Fe3O4NPs can react with H2O2to form · OH radicals over a broad range of pH. Conversely, in absence of ATP, the catalytic activity was observed at acidic pH which was found maximum at pH2 (Fig. 4b), which decreases with the increase of pH and ∼ 20% of Fig. 4. Temperature and pH dependent peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4NPs in presence and absence of ATP: a) Temperature was varied from20to80 ◦ C and the peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4NPs was measured in presence and absence of ATP. b) pH was varied from1to12and the peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4NPs was measured in presence and absence of ATP. The absorbance of oxidized TMB at652nm was measured after10min of incubation. Data expressed as SD calculated from three (n = 3) independent experiments. activity is retained at physiological pH. The peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4NPs was further compared with the HRP, a well-known natural peroxidase enzyme. The activity of HRP at pH7.4and at37 ◦ C was compared with the activity at60 ◦ C. As expected, the enzymatic activity was decreased ∼ 90% at60 ◦ C (Fig. 5and Fig. ESI4). This suggests that the enzyme-like activity of Fe3O4NPs is not compromised at higher temperatures, unlike natural HRP [2]. Most of the earlier studies revealed the catalytic oxidation of TMB with different NPs was found maximum at acidic pH than neutral and basic conditions [14,24,38,39]. Moreover, they displayed an optimum peroxidase activity at pH (4-5.5) and temperature (40-60 ◦ C) compared to HRP. This emphasizes a strong point that our system showed a comparative peroxidase-like activity over a broad range of pH and temperature, exhibiting a wide range of pH and thermal stability over native enzyme. # 3.5. Hydroxyl radicals mediate the peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4NPs Ascorbic acid participates in the neutralization or termination reactions with reactive oxygen species due to their antioxidant properties. Therefore, we used AA, a natural antioxidant to deter- Fig. 5. Catalytic activity comparison of Fe3O4NPs and HRP at higher temperatures: Peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4NPs and HRP was performed at37and60 ◦ C with suitable controls. The absorbance was captured after5min of incubation. Data expressed as SD calculated from three (n = 3) independent experiments. Fig. 6. Role of hydroxyl radicals on the peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4NPs: The peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4NPs was performed in presence of ascorbic acid (AA) and ATP. Data expressed as SD calculated from three (n = 3) independent experiments. mine the inhibitory effect on the peroxidase activity, which usually requires hydroxyl radicals to oxidize TMB. As expected, the addition of AA reduced the oxidation rate of TMB compared to the control (Fig. 6). Further earlier studies have also revealed the inhibitory activity of AA on peroxidase activity shown by peroxidase enzymes and nanoparticles [40,41]. So the study confirmed, that the synergistic peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4NPs and ATP are driven by reactive oxygen species like · OH radicals and are essential for the oxidation of TMB. # 3.6. Physiological pH peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4NPs can be exploited for one step glucose detection Translation of peroxidase activity of Fe3O4NPs at physiological pH for bio-sensing application was carried out by detecting glucose in a concentration range of0.05-4mM. It is well known that glucose oxidation at physiological pH is facilitated by GOx that reacts with glucose at pH7.4to produce D-glucono𝛅 -lactone and H2O2. Since the concentration of 𝛃 -D glucopyranose is higher at pH7.4and GOx is relatively more specific for 𝛃 -d -glucose, the glucose oxidation is usually carried out at physiological pH. Thus, typically Fig. 7. Translation of peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4NPs in a single step colorimetric detection of glucose: Detection of different concentrations of glucose (Glu) by the peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4NPs in presence of ATP at40 ◦ C (a) and60 ◦ C (b). Both of these temperatures show linear detection range (0.05-4mM) of glucose (c and d). The absorbance was measured after30min of incubation. Data expressed as SD calculated from three (n = 3) independent experiments. glucose sensing is carried out in two separate pH; the enzymatic reaction to produce H2O2at neutral pH followed by peroxidase reaction to oxidize the substrate (TMB) with H2O2at acidic pH. This limited the application of Fe3O4NPs in hassle-free translation into a device platform as two different reagent and pH buffers were needed. The enhanced peroxidase activity of Fe3O4NPs at physiological pH by careful modifications of buffer composition, concentration and synergistic action of ATP was used to design a single step colorimetric detection of glucose at pH7.4at two different temperatures40and60 ◦ C (Fig. 7). The results showed a glucose concentration dependent increase in absorbance of TMB at physiological pH in presence of ATP at both40and60 ◦ C. As evident from Fig. 7, the colorimetric detection increased for all the glucose concentrations and sensitivity increased at60 ◦ C compared to40 ◦ C (Fig. 7b and d). Earlier studies related to IONPs discussed for glucose detection mostly followed the two-step process [14,25,26,42-44], however, in this study we extended the peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4NPs from acidic to physiological pH and detected glucose with a minimum colorimetric detection of50 𝛍 M in one step at pH7.4. The selectivity of the assay towards glucose was also tested in presence of fructose. The assay did not show any oxidation of TMB in presence of fructose, suggesting that the assay is highly selective to glucose (Figure ESI5). The utility of this method was also tested to detect glucose level in human blood serum samples. The human blood serum was spiked with different concentrations of glucose followed by detection using above described method. Fig. 8clearly reveals that the physiologically relevant concentra- tions of glucose in blood can be easily monitored using a simple spectrophotometer. It was also observed that this one step method could detect the glucose from human blood serum at concentrations as low as0.0625mM within5minutes (Fig. 8a). We tested the physiological concentration range of glucose present in human blood serum after20times of dilution (0.0625-0.5mM). Fig. 8b shows the concentration dependent increase in absorbance of oxidized TMB during glucose monitoring (0.0625, 0.25and0.5mM) in human blood serum. Therefore, it can be concluded that this single step detection system can easily be applied for quick monitoring of blood glucose level, ranging from normal to diabetic persons. # 4. Conclusion In summary, the peroxidase activity of Fe3O4NPs was demonstrated at physiological pH in presence of ATP. This activity was preserved over a wide range of pH (2-11) and temperature, up to80 ◦ C. It is hypothesized that ATP forms a stable complex with Fe3O4NPs, which in presence of H2O2results in the generation of · OH radicals. Additionally, ATP may also stabilize the oxidized TMB radical cation by facilitating single electron transfer. Translation of the peroxidase activity at physiological pH was carried out by detection of glucose (0.05-4mM) in a single step with a colorimetric detection limit at50 𝛍 M. Further this finding was extended for detection of glucose level in human blood serum at a relevant range (0.0625-0.5mM), which showed that glucose could be detected in <5min at pH7.4. This single step detection method can also be Fig. 8. Singe step detection of glucose in human blood serum. a) Concentration and time dependent increase in absorbance of serum sample supplemented with0.0625, 0.25and0.5mM glucose (Glu) at37 ◦ C. b) Absorbance spectra showing increase in absorbance for different glucose concentrations in serum (0.0625, 0.25and0.5mM) after5min incubation at37 ◦ C. The representative images of color of TMB generated due to oxidation in presence of glucose is shown in figure inset. Data expressed as SD calculated from three (n = 3) independent experiments. conveniently applied to other complicated two-step detection systems, such as two-step bio-sensors. Moreover, one-step detection and operation at physiological pH will facilitate the easy translation of peroxidase activity of Fe3O4NPs into a device platform to create a point-of-care device. # Conflict of interest Authors declare no conflict of interest. # Acknowledgements The financial assistance for the Centre for Nanotechnology Research and Applications (CENTRA) by The Gujarat Institute of Chemical Technology (GICT) is acknowledged.
